content:
Why major JavaScript tools are being rewritten in Rust and what it means for the future of web development

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

For many years, JavaScript has been the language of the web. It powers browsers, frontend frameworks, and even backend systems through Node.js.
There is a famous idea in software development called Atwood’s Law, which says:
Any application that can be written in JavaScript will eventually be written in JavaScript.
At first, people laughed at the idea of using JavaScript everywhere. But today, it is no longer a joke. JavaScript is everywhere — from frontend apps to backend servers, build tools, and even desktop applications.
But something interesting is happening now.
A new trend is quietly emerging in the web development world:
All JavaScript applications are written in JavaScript… but all JavaScript tooling is slowly being rewritten in Rust.
This shift is changing how modern developer tools are built.

The Rise of Rust in Web Tooling
In recent years, major projects like Bun, parts of the Vite ecosystem, and many modern JavaScript build tools have started adopting Rust.
Now even Meta (Facebook) has joined this movement.
They recently migrated Flow, their JavaScript static type checker, from OCaml to Rust.
This is a big deal because Flow is not a small project. It is used internally across massive Meta codebases, including parts of React’s ecosystem.
So why move to Rust?
To understand that, we first need to understand Flow itself.

What Is Flow?
Flow is a static type checker for JavaScript created by Meta.
It was designed to make JavaScript safer by catching errors before code runs — similar to TypeScript.
In fact, Flow existed before TypeScript became the dominant standard.
For years, Flow helped large-scale JavaScript projects avoid bugs without switching away from JavaScript entirely.
However, over time, TypeScript became the industry standard, and Flow became less popular publicly — even though Meta still uses it internally.

Why Flow Was Originally Written in OCaml
Flow was built using OCaml, a language often used for compilers, type systems, and programming language tools.
OCaml is powerful because it makes it easier to build:

• Type checkers

• Parsers

• Compilers

• Static analysis tools
However, OCaml also has limitations in modern development environments:

• Smaller ecosystem

• Limited hiring pool

• Less integration with modern tooling

• Harder to scale with today’s JavaScript infrastructure
As JavaScript codebases grew larger inside Meta, Flow started facing serious performance and scalability challenges.

Why Meta Considered Rust
As early as 2019, Meta engineers explored rewriting Flow in Rust.
But at the time, it was considered too risky:

• It would take too long

• The benefits were uncertain

• Existing systems were still “just good enough”

• A full rewrite could introduce bugs
So instead of rewriting Flow, the team improved architecture and scalability in OCaml.
But the idea of Rust never disappeared.

The Turning Point: Why Rust Won
By 2025, things had changed dramatically.
Several major signals made Rust the obvious choice:
1. Internal Rust Success at Meta
Meta’s internal Rust-based projects (like Firefly, a Rust rewrite of Pyre) showed up to 30× performance improvements.
2. Industry Shift Toward Native Tooling
Other ecosystems were also moving away from JavaScript-based tooling:

• TypeScript team explored a Go-based native compiler

• SWC, Biome, Rollup, and Vite ecosystem increasingly adopted Rust
Rust was becoming the standard for high-performance developer tools.

Why Rust Instead of Go or Kotlin?
Meta also considered easier options like Go or Kotlin.
These languages would have been simpler because they use garbage collection, which fits compiler-style workloads.
But Rust had one major advantage:
It was becoming the core language of modern JavaScript tooling.
If Flow was going to be rewritten, it made sense to align it with the future ecosystem rather than the easiest path.
This meant choosing Rust — even though it is harder.

The Challenge of Rust
Unlike OCaml, Go, or Kotlin, Rust does not use garbage collection.
Instead, it uses:

• Ownership system

• Borrowing rules

• Explicit memory management
This makes Rust:

• Harder to learn

• Harder to port to

• More strict in design
But it also gives:

• Better performance

• Predictable memory usage

• Safer concurrency

• Zero-cost abstractions
For a large-scale tool like Flow, performance mattered more than convenience.

The Migration Strategy: No Rewrite, Only Porting
Meta did not fully rewrite Flow from scratch.
Instead, they chose a line-by-line porting approach.
Why?
Because Flow is critical infrastructure used across huge internal codebases. Any behavioral change could break thousands of projects.
So the goal was:
Keep behavior identical. Change only the implementation language.
This reduced risk and allowed gradual migration.

AI-Assisted Migration
One of the most interesting parts of the project is that AI played a major role.
The team used AI tools to:

• Convert OCaml functions into Rust

• Preserve exact behavior

• Speed up repetitive porting work
However, they did not allow AI to redesign logic.
The goal was not creativity — it was accuracy and consistency.
This approach made the migration much faster and more predictable.

The Migration Process
Step 1: Parser Migration
The parser was the first major component.

• Completed in about 4 weeks

• Fully AI-assisted

• Achieved ~2× performance improvement
This gave confidence to continue.

Step 2: Core System Porting
Next came:

• Module resolution

• Dependency analysis

• Type signature extraction
These systems formed the foundation of Flow.

Step 3: Type Checker Migration
This was the hardest part.
At first:
The Rust version passed zero tests
But because the port was mechanical, debugging was straightforward:

• Compare OCaml vs Rust outputs

• Fix differences one by one

• Gradually align behavior
Within about a week, tests started passing.

Performance Challenges After Migration
Even after successful migration, the Rust version was not immediately faster.
In fact, in some cases it was slower.
Why?
Because:

• OCaml logic was not optimized for Rust patterns

• Reference counting (Rc) and RefCell caused memory overhead

• Some OCaml structures translated poorly into Rust

• New inefficiencies were introduced during porting
The team had to optimize carefully:

• Fix memory cycles

• Improve scheduling

• Remove redundant computations

• Refactor allocation-heavy patterns

Final Results
After optimization, the Rust version of Flow achieved:

• ~2× overall performance improvement

• 30%–100% faster execution in heavy type-checking workloads

• Better scalability for large JavaScript monorepos
More importantly:
Flow became aligned with the modern Rust-based tooling ecosystem.

Why This Matters for Web Development
This migration is part of a bigger trend:
JavaScript is still the language of apps
But…
Rust is becoming the language of tools
We are seeing a clear split:

• JavaScript → Applications

• Rust → Build tools, compilers, and infrastructure
This is already visible in:

• Bun runtime

• Vite ecosystem tools

• SWC compiler

• Biome formatter

• Meta’s internal tooling

• A Fun Historical Fact

Rust itself has an interesting origin story:

• Rust was originally started in 2006 by Graydon Hoare

• Its first compiler was written in OCaml

• Mozilla later adopted and developed it further

• Rust became self-hosting around 2011
So in a way:
OCaml helped build Rust, and now Rust is replacing OCaml in modern tooling.

Final Thoughts
The migration of Flow from OCaml to Rust is more than just a language change.
It represents a broader shift in software engineering:

• Performance matters more than ever

• Developer tooling is becoming systems-level software

• Rust is emerging as the backbone of modern infrastructure
We are entering a new era where:
JavaScript builds the apps — but Rust builds the tools that build the apps.
